refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—enabling visibility and traceability of energy efficiency data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a performance acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement; it depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational context beyond the FR itself. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the measurement infrastructure; meeting the target is validated through acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved) during operation, computes energy_per_ton, and reports results in a format accessible to the Site Manager and System Engineer for comparison against the customer-defined E_target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall acquire and log energy consumption data during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall acquire and log work output (tonnage moved) data during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute energy_per_ton as the ratio of cumulative energy consumed to cumulative tonnage moved.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall report energy_per_ton metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, System Engineer) in a human-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period; report against target.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors; stream data to edge aggregator; compute energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold breached; archive for post-period reporting.
DP3: Decompose energy consumption into sub-processes (transport, loading, unloading); measure energy and tonnage per sub-process; aggregate ratios; report composite and per-process efficiency.
DP4: Establish baseline energy_per_ton from historical operations; measure current period; compute delta; report variance against target as proxy for efficiency.
DP5: Instrument energy at multiple points (engine, drivetrain, auxiliary systems); weight contributions by operational mode; compute blended energy_per_ton; report with mode-specific breakdowns.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period; report against target.

potential_sources: Energy measurement and tonnage measurement are logically independent; either can be replaced without affecting the other.
Offline computation decouples measurement from reporting; allows flexible analysis window.
No implicit dependency on real-time systems, edge computing, or sub-process decomposition.
Measurement points (source and delivery) are physically separated; minimal cross-coupling of instrumentation.
Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly satisfied by the DP without intermediate transformations.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low coupling because it isolates three independent concerns: (1) energy quantification, (2) work quantification, (3) ratio computation and comparison. Each can be implemented, validated, and modified independently. DP2 and DP5 introduce real-time streaming and mode-dependent weighting, increasing coupling to operational state and sensor synchronization. DP3 couples efficiency reporting to process decomposition, risking scope creep. DP4 introduces historical baseline dependency, complicating acceptance criteria validation. DP1 is the simplest, most decoupled solution.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is metered or can be instrumented at a single point (e.g., fuel tank, electrical panel). RISK: Distributed or multi-source energy systems may require multiple measurement points, increasing complexity.

ASSUMPTION: Tonnage moved is unambiguously defined and measurable at delivery point (e.g., scale, manifest count). RISK: Ambiguity in what constitutes 'work output' (partial loads, rejected material, return trips) could invalidate ratio.
ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is fixed and known in advance. RISK: If period is variable or overlaps operational cycles, ratio computation may not align with business intent.

ASSUMPTION: Energy and tonnage data are available with sufficient accuracy and temporal alignment for offline computation. RISK: Measurement lag, calibration drift, or data loss could compromise ratio validity.
ASSUMPTION: Offline post-period reporting is acceptable for decision-making. RISK: If real-time feedback is required for operational adjustment, DP1 does not support it.
ASSUMPTION: No sub-process-level efficiency optimization is required. RISK: If customer later demands per-phase efficiency (e.g., loading vs. transport), DP1 may not provide sufficient granularity.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not mandate continuous monitoring or real-time alerts. RISK: If safety regulations require live energy/load tracking, DP1 may be insufficient.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target, with measurement points at energy source and work delivery, computed offline over a defined evaluation period, and reported as a single aggregate ratio. Acceptance criteria: Measured energy_per_ton = (total_energy_used / total_tonnage_moved) ≤ E_target, where measurements are taken over the agreed evaluation period with ±5% accuracy tolerance. Constraints: Measurement must not interfere with worksite safety plan or operational policies; data collection must be non-intrusive and compatible with existing equipment.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level energy efficiency monitoring and reporting; aggregates instrumentation data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; computes and reports energy-to-work ratios against operational targets.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives fuel/electricity consumption data from Vehicle and work output (tonnage moved) telemetry from point of delivery.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Reports efficiency metrics and target variance to operational planning and task optimization systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Flags anomalous energy consumption patterns that may indicate safety or operational faults.

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong containment: offline post-evaluation computation, aggregation, and reporting are system-level concerns. Instrumentation logic (source and delivery point) is distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation, but ratio computation and target comparison are naturally owned by NGHE Solution. Minor diffusion: requires Telemetry ingestion from Vehicle and Teleoperation; SafetyAlert and Tasking outputs are appropriate system-level exports.

Weak containment: Vehicle owns fuel/electricity instrumentation at source, but work output instrumentation occurs at point of delivery (outside Vehicle scope). Ratio computation and offline reporting are system-level concerns, not vehicle-local. High diffusion: would require Vehicle to export raw energy data and import work output from external source, then perform cross-domain computation.

Weak containment: Teleoperation may instrument work output at delivery point, but fuel/electricity instrumentation resides in Vehicle. Offline post-evaluation and ratio computation are not teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: would require bidirectional coupling to Vehicle for energy data and system-level reporting infrastructure.

Poor containment: Safety Services is not the natural owner of energy efficiency monitoring or work output instrumentation. Ratio computation and target reporting are outside safety domain. Very high diffusion: would require Safety Services to aggregate data from Vehicle and Teleoperation, compute efficiency metrics, and manage reporting—violating separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally a system-level energy efficiency monitoring and reporting function. Instrumentation at source (Vehicle) and delivery point (Teleoperation) are distributed, but aggregation, offline computation, and target-based reporting are cohesive system responsibilities. NGHE Solution is the appropriate boundary because it owns cross-subsystem metrics, offline analysis, and operational reporting.
containment_risks: Instrumentation logic is split across Vehicle and Teleoperation; NGHE Solution depends on both subsystems providing consistent, timestamped telemetry.
Offline post-evaluation period definition and data retention policy must be clearly specified to avoid ambiguity about when computation occurs and how long data is buffered.
Target definition and update mechanism must be externally managed (likely via Tasking interface); NGHE Solution should not own target policy.
Potential latency between energy input and work output measurement; temporal alignment and causality inference must be addressed in the DP refinement.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP1 is well-aligned with NGHE Solution ownership. However, refine the DP to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Instrument energy input at Vehicle source and export via Telemetry; (2) Instrument work output at delivery point (Teleoperation) and export via Telemetry; (3) NGHE Solution aggregates both streams, computes ratio offline post-evaluation period, and reports via Tasking and SafetyAlert. This clarifies that instrumentation is distributed but aggregation and reporting are centralized, improving modularity and testability.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 correctly maps to FR by specifying the complete measurement and reporting workflow: instrument energy input at source, instrument work output at delivery point, compute ratio offline post-evaluation, and report against target. The DP is solution-agnostic and logically sound, addressing all functional requirements without prescribing implementation details prematurely.

diffusion_risks: Instrumentation logic is distributed across Vehicle (energy input at source) and Teleoperation (work output at delivery point); NGHE Solution depends on both subsystems providing consistent, timestamped telemetry. Inconsistent timestamps or data gaps will degrade ratio accuracy.

Telemetry ingestion interface must guarantee data ordering and completeness; if Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems drop or delay telemetry packets, NGHE Solution's offline computation will be unreliable. No error handling or data validation strategy is specified.

Offline post-evaluation period definition is implicit; ambiguity exists about buffer retention policy, computation trigger timing, and whether partial evaluation periods are supported. This must be explicitly specified to avoid implementation drift.

Target definition and update mechanism are external (via Tasking); NGHE Solution must support dynamic target changes without service interruption. Coupling to external target management introduces a secondary dependency.

Temporal alignment between energy input measurement (Vehicle) and work output measurement (Teleoperation) is not addressed. If measurements are asynchronous or causally misaligned, ratio computation may be invalid.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves strong containment (0.85) for aggregation, offline computation, and reporting—the core system-level concerns. Instrumentation is appropriately distributed (Vehicle owns energy source instrumentation; Teleoperation owns delivery-point work output instrumentation), avoiding duplication. However, the DP->IM bridge requires explicit interface contracts: (1) Telemetry must define schema, timestamp precision, and delivery guarantees for energy and tonnage streams; (2) Tasking must define target update protocol and reporting frequency; (3) SafetyAlert must define anomaly thresholds and escalation rules. Without these contracts, diffusion risk increases due to implicit assumptions about data quality and timing.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period' is directly verifiable if: (1) energy_used is measured in consistent units (kWh or fuel volume) at Vehicle source; (2) ton_moved is measured in consistent units at delivery point; (3) offline computation aggregates both streams over a well-defined evaluation period; (4) E_target is externally provided and immutable during evaluation. NGHE Solution must log all intermediate computations (raw energy, raw tonnage, aggregation timestamps, ratio, target comparison) to enable audit and root-cause analysis. Verification requires access to telemetry logs and computation records.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle owns energy instrumentation; Teleoperation owns work output instrumentation; NGHE Solution owns aggregation, ratio computation, and reporting. However, allocation clarity is weakened by three gaps: (1) No explicit owner for offline post-evaluation period definition and data retention policy (recommend: Tasking/operational planning); (2) No explicit owner for target definition and update mechanism (recommend: external Tasking system); (3) No explicit owner for temporal alignment and causality inference between energy and work output (recommend: NGHE Solution DP refinement must specify alignment strategy, e.g., time-window aggregation or event-based correlation). Recommend adding a fourth IM boundary (Operational Planning or Tasking) to own target and evaluation period policies, decoupling NGHE Solution from policy management.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP1 to explicitly separate instrumentation, aggregation, and reporting concerns: (1) Vehicle instruments energy input at source and exports timestamped energy telemetry; (2) Teleoperation instruments work output at delivery point and exports timestamped tonnage telemetry; (3) NGHE Solution aggregates both streams over offline post-evaluation period, computes energy_per_ton ratio, and reports against externally-provided E_target via Tasking and SafetyAlert interfaces.

Define Telemetry interface contract: specify energy units (kWh or fuel volume), tonnage units (metric tons), timestamp precision (millisecond or better), delivery guarantee (at-least-once or exactly-once), and error handling (missing data, out-of-order packets, duplicate timestamps).

Define offline post-evaluation period: specify trigger condition (e.g., end of shift, end of week, manual request), buffer retention policy (how long raw telemetry is retained), and computation latency SLA (e.g., results available within 1 hour of period close).

Define target update mechanism: specify whether E_target is static per evaluation period or dynamic, how target changes are communicated to NGHE Solution, and whether in-flight computations are affected by target updates.

Address temporal alignment: specify whether energy and tonnage measurements are synchronized (e.g., GPS-based time sync) or asynchronous, and define aggregation strategy (e.g., time-window bucketing, event-based correlation) to ensure causally valid ratio computation.

Add SafetyAlert threshold definition: specify what constitutes 'anomalous energy consumption' (e.g., >2σ deviation from baseline, sudden spikes, sustained over-consumption) and escalation rules (alert to operator, log to safety system, trigger diagnostic).
Allocate target and evaluation period ownership to an external IM boundary (e.g., Operational Planning or Tasking system), decoupling NGHE Solution from policy management and reducing coupling.

Specify audit and logging requirements: NGHE Solution must log all raw telemetry ingestion, aggregation steps, ratio computation, and target comparison results to enable post-evaluation verification and root-cause analysis of efficiency variances.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR->DP, DP->IM, FR->IM) pass, but the gate results expose critical diffusion risks and allocation ambiguities that must be resolved before decomposition proceeds. The DP is logically sound but insufficiently detailed to guide safe implementation. Seven recommended revisions address: (1) instrumentation distribution and telemetry interface contracts, (2) offline post-evaluation period definition, (3) target update mechanism, (4) temporal alignment strategy, (5) SafetyAlert thresholds, (6) ownership allocation for external dependencies, and (7) audit/logging requirements. Given high safety criticality, these gaps represent unacceptable risk if carried forward to sub-level decomposition. Revising the current DP to incorporate explicit interface contracts, temporal alignment strategy, and ownership boundaries will eliminate diffusion risk and provide a stable foundation for next-level decomposition.

revise.why: DP1 must be refined to explicitly address: (1) Telemetry interface contract (energy units, tonnage units, timestamp precision, delivery guarantee, error handling); (2) Offline post-evaluation period trigger, buffer retention, and computation latency SLA; (3) Target update mechanism (static vs. dynamic, communication protocol, impact on in-flight computations); (4) Temporal alignment strategy (synchronized vs. asynchronous measurements, aggregation method for causally valid ratio computation); (5) SafetyAlert threshold definition and escalation rules; (6) Audit and logging requirements for verification and root-cause analysis. These refinements must be incorporated into DP1 before decomposition to sub-level FRs, ensuring that implementation modules inherit clear, unambiguous requirements and interface contracts.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystem can instrument energy input at source with millisecond-precision timestamps and export telemetry reliably.
Teleoperation subsystem can instrument work output (tonnage) at delivery point with millisecond-precision timestamps and export telemetry reliably.
Tasking system provides E_target externally and manages evaluation period definitions; NGHE Solution consumes these as immutable inputs during offline computation.
SafetyAlert system is available to receive anomaly reports and escalate to operators; NGHE Solution defines thresholds and triggers.
Offline post-evaluation period is well-defined operationally (e.g., end of shift, end of week) and communicated to NGHE Solution before computation begins.
Energy and tonnage measurements can be temporally aligned (either synchronized or aggregated over time windows) to ensure causally valid ratio computation.
Audit and logging infrastructure is available to capture raw telemetry, aggregation steps, and computation results for post-evaluation verification.
open_risks: Telemetry delivery guarantee not specified: if Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems drop or delay packets, NGHE Solution's offline computation will be unreliable. Recommend: define at-least-once or exactly-once delivery semantics and implement deduplication/ordering logic.

Temporal misalignment between energy input (Vehicle) and work output (Teleoperation) measurements: if measurements are asynchronous or causally misaligned, ratio computation may be invalid. Recommend: specify synchronization method (GPS-based time sync, NTP, or event-based correlation) and aggregation strategy (time-window bucketing or event-based correlation).

Offline post-evaluation period definition is implicit: ambiguity about buffer retention policy, computation trigger timing, and partial evaluation period support. Recommend: explicitly define trigger condition, buffer retention SLA, and computation latency SLA.

Target update mechanism not specified: if E_target changes during evaluation period, unclear whether in-flight computations are affected. Recommend: specify whether E_target is static per evaluation period or dynamic, and define update protocol.

SafetyAlert thresholds not defined: unclear what constitutes 'anomalous energy consumption' or when to escalate. Recommend: define thresholds (e.g., >2σ deviation, sudden spikes, sustained over-consumption) and escalation rules.

Ownership of offline post-evaluation period and target definition not allocated: NGHE Solution may become tightly coupled to operational planning. Recommend: allocate to external IM boundary (Tasking or Operational Planning).

Audit and logging requirements not specified: verification and root-cause analysis may be compromised if intermediate computations are not logged. Recommend: specify logging schema and retention policy.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: energy units, tonnage units, timestamp precision, delivery guarantee, error handling protocol, and schema definition.
Offline post-evaluation period specification: trigger condition, buffer retention policy, computation latency SLA, and support for partial evaluation periods.
Target update protocol: specification of whether E_target is static or dynamic, communication mechanism, and impact on in-flight computations.

Temporal alignment strategy: specification of synchronization method (GPS-based, NTP, event-based) and aggregation method (time-window bucketing, event-based correlation) to ensure causally valid ratio computation.
SafetyAlert threshold definition: specification of anomaly detection criteria (e.g., statistical deviation, spike detection, sustained over-consumption) and escalation rules.

Ownership allocation for external dependencies: confirmation that Tasking or Operational Planning system owns target and evaluation period definitions, decoupling NGHE Solution from policy management.

Audit and logging specification: schema for logging raw telemetry ingestion, aggregation steps, ratio computation, and target comparison results; retention policy and access controls for verification and root-cause analysis.
Verification test plan: acceptance criteria for energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period, including test cases for data quality, temporal alignment, and anomaly detection.
